1.synthesis: The threshing floor encounter is Naomi’s calculated initiative to secure Ruth’s covenant household — the mānôaḥ she had prayed for at the parting in chapter 1. Her tactical instructions (wash, anoint, dress, go down at night, note where Boaz lies, uncover his feet, lie down) reflect sophisticated social knowledge: the harvest feast would leave Boaz satisfied and resting; the threshing floor was a communal space, not a private chamber; uncovering his feet and lying at them was a recognized posture of petition, not merely romantic encounter. Ruth’s response — “All that you tell me I will do” (v5) — extends her covenant compliance from following Naomi in chapter 1 to following her specific instruction here. Ruth’s petition to Boaz (v9: “Spread your kānāp̄ over your servant, for you are a go’el”) uses the same word Boaz used in his blessing in Ruth 2:12 — creating the book’s central theological move: she asks the man who prayed for YHWH’s wings to become the answer to his own prayer.
1.historical_context: The threshing floor (gōren) was typically on an elevated, exposed hillside where the prevailing westerly wind could separate grain from chaff after harvest. Sleeping on the threshing floor to guard stored grain from theft was standard practice. Naomi’s instruction to anoint and dress well signals a transition from mourning garments — widows in the ancient world wore distinctive clothing during mourning periods — to readiness for a new covenant household. The spreading of a garment over a woman (kānāp̄, v9 — also translated “wing” or “corner of garment”) was the legal gesture of claiming a woman as a wife, attested in Ezekiel 16:8 where YHWH spreads his garment over Israel in the covenant-making act. Ruth positions herself as a petitioner seeking a specific legal act from Boaz — not a social visit but a formal go’el petition.
1.literary_structure: The section follows a commission-execution structure: Naomi’s instructions (vv1-5) followed by Ruth’s exact compliance (vv6-7) followed by the midnight encounter (vv8-9). The dramatic midnight timing echoes decisive divine action: the Exodus death of the firstborn at midnight (Exodus 12:29), Samuel’s night call (1 Samuel 3:3). Boaz’s discovery — he startles, turns over, finds a woman at his feet, asks “Who are you?” — is genuinely surprising: the encounter is not a pre-arranged liaison but an unexpected petition. Ruth’s request in verse 9 is simultaneously a legal go’el petition, a marriage proposal, and the enactment of YHWH’s own wing-imagery through a human agent — the book’s central typological moment.
1.theological_application: The threshing floor is Israel’s primary metaphor for judgment and sorting — the image John the Baptist invokes for the Messiah’s coming (Matthew 3:12: “his winnowing fork is in his hand, and he will clear his threshing floor”). Ruth approaches the one who will become her redeemer on the very ground where the winnowing of grain from chaff takes place. The petition she makes — “you are a go’el” — appeals not to Boaz’s arbitrary generosity but to his kinship obligation: covenant grounds, not sentiment. This legal clarity models how covenant petitioners approach YHWH — not begging for arbitrary mercy but appealing to the covenant commitment that binds the redeemer to the one who needs redemption.
1.christology: Ruth’s request — “Spread your wing over your servant” — is the book’s central typological moment. Boaz blessed Ruth in Ruth 2:12 with the prayer that YHWH would reward her under whose wings she had taken refuge; now she asks him to be those wings. In Ezekiel 16:8, YHWH himself spreads his kānāp̄ over Israel in the covenant-making act: the protective covering of YHWH over his people is the model behind both Boaz’s blessing and Ruth’s petition. Christ’s lament over Jerusalem — “How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings” (Matthew 23:37) — uses the same image. The go’el who spreads his wing over the widow is the human form of YHWH’s own covenant gesture; in Christ, YHWH’s protective covering takes flesh definitively.

10.synthesis: Boaz’s response to Ruth’s petition contains three elements: recognition, promise, and provision. He recognizes her “latter hesed” as greater than the former (v10) — she has chosen the go’el route rather than pursuing younger men, prioritizing the dead man’s name over her own advantage. He promises unconditionally to act, invoking YHWH’s own name as surety — “As YHWH lives, I will redeem you” (v13) — while honestly disclosing the legal complication of a nearer kinsman. He provides six measures of barley as both practical provision and symbolic earnest of his intention, sending Ruth back to Naomi with grain and the implicit message that the matter will be resolved today. Naomi reads the grain correctly (v17) and closes the chapter with a word of assured faith: “the man will not rest until he has settled the matter today.”
10.historical_context: Boaz’s oath “As YHWH lives” (v13) was the most binding oath form available in ancient Israel, invoking the deity as witness and guarantor — the same formula appears in 1 Samuel 14:45 and 2 Samuel 15:21. Six measures of barley (likely seʾāh, about 8 liters each) totaled approximately 37 kg — a substantial load almost too heavy for one person to carry, and a signal to Naomi that Boaz’s intent is serious. His instruction for Ruth to leave before dawn (v14: “before one person could recognize another”) is social protection for her reputation: the encounter, while legally and morally legitimate, could be misread before the gate proceedings validated it. Boaz’s praise of Ruth as a “woman of valor” (ʾēšet ḥayil, v11) uses the same ḥayil applied to himself in Ruth 2:1 — the narrator pairs them in character before their legal union is formalized.
10.literary_structure: The section mirrors the chapter 1 departure scene: Naomi released Ruth from covenant obligation; Boaz now accepts it. Ruth’s “lie here until morning” becomes “rise before dawn”; Naomi’s earlier release becomes Boaz’s acceptance. The six measures of barley function narratively to transfer information from threshing floor to town: Naomi reads the grain correctly as Boaz’s message, and her confidence — “he will not rest until he has settled the matter today” — uses the šāqaṭ root (rest/settle) related to the book’s key word mānôaḥ: the man cannot find šāqaṭ until he has provided mānôaḥ for Ruth. The reversal of rest-vocabulary in verse 18 is the narrator’s signal that the chapter’s promised rest is about to arrive.
10.theological_application: Boaz’s oath by YHWH’s life — securing his commitment on divine authority — establishes the certainty of his redemptive intention. The complication of the nearer kinsman does not diminish the oath but clarifies its conditions: if the legal order is respected, Boaz will redeem. His commitment to follow the legal process rather than shortcutting it demonstrates that true hesed operates through covenant law, not around it. The earnest provision — six measures of barley sent back to Naomi — is the tangible pledge of intent: not words alone but grain in hand, provision now as first installment of the fullness to come. Naomi’s confident reading of the grain as message models the faith of those who can read YHWH’s provision correctly: the redeemer’s urgency is the ground of patient waiting.
10.christology: Boaz’s oath — “As YHWH lives, I will redeem you” — secured on YHWH’s own life, finds its ultimate expression in the Hebrews argument that Christ’s priesthood is permanent “because he continues forever” (Hebrews 7:24). The certainty of Christ’s redemption is secured not on anything that might change or perish but on the indestructible life of God. The six measures of barley Boaz gives before the transaction closes are the type of the Spirit given as “pledge (arrabōn) of our inheritance until we acquire full possession of it” (Ephesians 1:14; 2 Corinthians 1:22). Naomi’s confident “he will not rest until he has settled the matter today” is the faith of those who know the redeemer’s urgency: “he who began a good work in you will bring it to completion” (Philippians 1:6).
